---
title: "Spinoza Verification and Proof-Carrying Claims"
chapter_id: "spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "spinoza"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "verification_bandwidth"
  - "treellm"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Spinoza Verification and Proof-Carrying Claims

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `spinoza`, `genesiscode`, `coherence_exchange`, `verification_bandwidth`, `treellm` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `spinoza`; supporting: `genesiscode`, `coherence_exchange`, `verification_bandwidth`, `treellm` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `spinoza`, `genesiscode`, `coherence_exchange`, `verification_bandwidth`, `treellm`; raw cache: `spinoza`, `genesiscode`, `verification_bandwidth`, `treellm`; connector/recovery: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

The stack needs a way to move selected claims from generated prose toward auditable, proof-carrying objects.

Claim ledgers store epistemic state. Spinoza defines the verification lane that can move selected claims toward stronger support tiers. Its key separation is proposer versus verifier: neural systems can propose hypotheses, proof sketches, citations, or formalization attempts, but a separate structured process decides whether the claim's tier is justified.

The chapter is deliberately bounded. It does not assume open-domain autoformalization is solved. It defines how the stack handles proof, justification, timeout, mismatch, downgrade, and escalation.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Neural generation alone cannot guarantee semantic consistency, source grounding, or formal validity.

A model can propose a formal-looking argument that does not mean what the original claim meant. It can cite sources that do not support the claim. It can produce a proof sketch with a hidden gap. It can time out and still sound confident. These are not rare edge cases; they are the normal boundary between generation and verification.

GenesisCode contributes the obligation-envelope view: accepted artifacts should carry tests, proofs, provenance, or capability policies. Verification Bandwidth adds that high-risk claims may need stronger verification rather than more context.

## Core Claim

[spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Spinoza-style verification should convert high-value claims into proof-carrying or justification-carrying artifacts with explicit tiers.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture, GenesisCode, The Coherence Exchange, Verification Bandwidth in Bounded Contexts, and TreeLLM - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a Proof-Carrying Claim.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Claim record"] --> B["Required tier"]
  B --> C["Interpretation mapping"]
  C --> D["Justification artifact"]
  D --> E{"Verifier result"}
  E -- "pass" --> F["Promote within tier"]
  E -- "fail" --> G["Downgrade / block"]
  E -- "timeout / mismatch" --> H["Residual / tribunal"]
  F --> I["Claim ledger update"]
  G --> I
  H --> I
```

- Separate heuristic proposal from symbolic or structured interrogation.
- Attach formal interpretation mappings, proof bundles, or citation-backed dossiers.
- Downgrade or escalate claims that cannot meet their required tier.

A proof-carrying claim contains the claim, required tier, interpretation mapping, justification artifact, verifier, verifier result, limitations, downgrade rule, and residuals. Formal proof is one tier, not the default expectation for every claim.

The most important behavior is downgrade discipline. Failed, timed-out, or mismatched verification should never become a promotional story. It should lower the support state, block promotion, or route the claim to tribunal review.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Proof-Carrying Claim.

- Claim ledger stores proof status.
- GenesisCode and Lean provide formal hooks.
- UAT provides adversarial review.

Minimum fields:

- `proof_claim_id`
- `claim_id`
- `required_tier`
- `interpretation_mapping`
- `justification_artifact`
- `verifier`
- `verifier_result`
- `limitations`
- `downgrade_rule`
- `tribunal_ref`
- `ledger_update`

The claim ledger stores proof status. GenesisCode and Lean provide formal hooks where the claim is formal enough. UAT handles adversarial review where the mapping, evidence, or stakes require multiple reviewers.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Proof tier is explicit.
- A justification artifact is required for promoted claims.
- Unsupported claims remain marked speculative or blocked.

The tier invariant is strict: a claim cannot occupy a formal or verified tier merely because a proof was attempted. It needs a justification artifact appropriate to the tier and a recorded verifier result.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Certified delusion.
- Invalid formalization.
- Justification artifact missing.

Certified delusion happens when the artifact looks formal but certifies the wrong thing. Invalid formalization means the mapping between natural-language claim and formal object is untrusted. Missing justification means the tier is unsupported. Each failure should downgrade or block the claim.

## Minimal implementation

A tiered proof-carrying claim schema with one non-philosophical invariant example.

The smallest useful implementation is a `proof_carrying_claim` schema and fixture that references an existing claim, a required tier, a justification artifact, a verifier result, limitations, and a ledger update. The fixture can show a planned proof target or local validation reference without pretending a theorem exists.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Proof artifact presence test | Check that promoted formal or verified claims carry an appropriate artifact reference. | planned; not run |
| Tier assignment test | Check that support tiers match verifier result and limitations. | planned; not run |
| Formalization mismatch review | Check that claims with uncertain interpretation mapping route to residual or tribunal review. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:spinoza.proof_carrying.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ProofCarryingClaims` | A claim at a formal support tier carries a valid proof or justification artifact reference. | planned |
| `lean:spinoza.proof_carrying.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ProofCarryingClaims` | A failed verifier result downgrades or blocks the claim rather than promoting it. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `verification_bandwidth` | Verification Bandwidth in Bounded Contexts | context_verification_theory | Generation vs verification; long context is not automatically long-range reasoning. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `treellm` | TreeLLM | semantic_representation | Hierarchical semantic token system for grounded, efficient, explainable language modeling. | source note available; local raw cache available |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Spinoza Verification and Proof-Carrying Claims turns high-value claims into auditable objects. It separates proposal from verification, names the required tier, attaches justification artifacts, records verifier results, and downgrades or escalates failures.

The chapter's conservative stance is essential: proof-carrying architecture is valuable only when failed proofs, verifier timeouts, and formalization mismatches lower confidence instead of decorating it.
